state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
product_description: AI document extraction pipeline for invoices, receipts, and contracts with OCR, NER, table extraction, and structured JSON output. REST API with batch processing.

plan.description: Initialize project structure and dependency manifest for the document_extraction solution. Set up Python virtual environment with OCR (Tesseract/EasyOCR), NER (spaCy/transformers), table extraction (camelot/pdfplumber), and FastAPI dependencies. Configure solution YAML files (project.yaml, prompts.yaml, tasks.yaml).
acceptance_criteria: All dependencies install cleanly with no version conflicts
project.yaml declares solution name, active_modules, and agent roles
prompts.yaml defines analyst, developer, and data_scientist roles with domain-specific system prompts
tasks.yaml enumerates all task types used by this solution
.env.example documents all required environment variables

plan.description: Design the OpenAPI specification for the document extraction REST API. Define endpoints for single document upload, batch submission, job status polling, and result retrieval. Specify request/response schemas for all three document types (invoice, receipt, contract).

endpoints: POST /extract — single document upload, returns job_id
POST /extract/batch — multipart upload of multiple docs, returns batch_id
GET /batches/{batch_id} — poll batch status with per-document breakdown
GET /batches/{batch_id}/results — retrieve all results in batch
payload.output_format: structured JSON with document_type, extracted_entities, tables, raw_text, confidence_scores, processing_metadata

acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI 3.1 spec (openapi.yaml) is complete and valid (passes swagger-cli validate)
All request bodies define required fields, optional fields, and file MIME type constraints
All response schemas include a top-level 'status' field (queued | processing | completed | failed)
Error responses follow RFC 7807 Problem Details format
Batch endpoint spec supports 1–100 documents per request

plan.description: Design and implement the database schema for job tracking, extraction results, and audit history. Use SQLAlchemy ORM with Alembic migrations. Tables: jobs, batch_jobs, extraction_results, entity_records, table_records.

tables.jobs: id, batch_id (FK nullable), document_type, status, filename, file_hash, created_at, updated_at, processing_time_ms
tables.batch_jobs: id, total_docs, completed_docs, failed_docs, status, created_at, completed_at
tables.extraction_results: id, job_id (FK), raw_text, confidence_score, ocr_engine, model_version, created_at
tables.entity_records: id, job_id (FK), entity_type, entity_value, start_char, end_char, confidence
tables.table_records: id, job_id (FK), table_index, headers (JSONB), rows (JSONB), page_number

acceptance_criteria: Alembic migration runs cleanly on a fresh PostgreSQL 15 instance
All foreign key constraints are enforced
Indexes exist on jobs.status, jobs.batch_id, entity_records.job_id, and entity_records.entity_type
JSONB columns for table rows are validated by a CHECK constraint against a JSON schema
Rollback migration returns schema to prior state without data loss

plan.description: Build the document ingestion and preprocessing pipeline. Handles PDF-to-image conversion, image normalization (deskew, denoise, contrast enhancement), file format validation, and document-type classification. Implements the multi_agent_sequential pattern: classifier → OCR → NER → table extractor → JSON assembler.

pipeline_stages: ingest: validate file type (PDF/PNG/JPG/TIFF), compute SHA-256 hash, store to object storage
preprocess: pdf2image conversion at 300 DPI, deskew via OpenCV, adaptive thresholding
classify: lightweight CNN or zero-shot LLM classifier → invoice | receipt | contract
ocr: route to engine based on doc quality score
ner: pass OCR text to domain NER model
table_extract: detect and parse tabular regions
assemble: merge all outputs into canonical JSON schema
payload.state_passing: Pydantic DocumentState object carries raw bytes → text → entities → tables across stages
payload.error_propagation: Each stage emits stage_name, status, error_code on failure; downstream stages skip gracefully

acceptance_criteria: Pipeline processes a 10-page PDF invoice end-to-end in under 30 seconds on a 2-vCPU machine
DocumentState is serializable to JSON at every stage boundary for debugging
Classification accuracy ≥ 90% on a 100-document labeled test set
Pipeline emits structured logs (JSON) for every stage transition
SHA-256 deduplication prevents reprocessing identical files within the same batch

plan.description: Integrate and configure the OCR engine layer. Support dual-engine strategy: Tesseract 5 (fast, offline) for clean scans and EasyOCR (deep-learning, GPU-optional) for degraded or handwritten documents. Implement quality-score-based routing. Output: raw text with bounding-box coordinates and per-word confidence scores.

engines.tesseract: pytesseract with OEM 3 (LSTM), PSM 6, language packs: eng + any configured locales
engines.easyocr: EasyOCR 1.7+ with GPU detection, batch inference for multi-page docs

payload.quality_scorer: Laplacian variance + histogram entropy → normalized 0–1 score

acceptance_criteria: Tesseract achieves ≥ 95% character accuracy on a clean-scan benchmark set
EasyOCR achieves ≥ 85% character accuracy on a degraded-scan benchmark set
Quality-score router correctly selects the appropriate engine for 90% of test cases
OCR layer is encapsulated behind an OCREngine abstract base class — engines are swappable
Bounding box coordinates are normalized to [0,1] relative to page dimensions
Bias evaluation performed
Model card generated

plan.description: Build the Named Entity Recognition (NER) model layer for extracting structured fields from invoices (vendor, total, line items, tax, due date), receipts (merchant, items, subtotal, date, payment method), and contracts (parties, effective date, termination clauses, governing law). Use a fine-tuned BERT-based model (layoutlm-v3 for layout-aware extraction) with a fallback to spaCy rule-based patterns.

payload.primary_model: microsoft/layoutlmv3-base fine-tuned on FUNSD + custom labeled invoices/receipts/contracts
payload.fallback: spaCy en_core_web_trf with custom EntityRuler patterns per document type

acceptance_criteria: LayoutLMv3 achieves F1 ≥ 0.88 on invoice NER test set (100 labeled documents)
LayoutLMv3 achieves F1 ≥ 0.85 on receipt NER test set
LayoutLMv3 achieves F1 ≥ 0.80 on contract NER test set
spaCy fallback activates when primary model confidence < threshold for all entities
NER layer accepts bounding-box-enriched OCR tokens (LayoutLM format) and plain text
Model version is recorded in every extraction_result row
Bias evaluation performed
Model card generated

plan.description: Implement the table extraction module for detecting and parsing tabular structures in invoices and receipts (line item tables) and contracts (schedule/exhibit tables). Use pdfplumber for native PDF tables and a TATR (Table Transformer) model for image-based table detection. Output canonical row/column JSON with header inference.

payload.pdf_native: pdfplumber — extracts tables from PDFs with embedded text layer
payload.routing: if PDF has text layer → pdfplumber else → TATR pipeline
payload.header_inference: First row heuristic + column value type analysis (date, currency, quantity)

acceptance_criteria: pdfplumber correctly parses ≥ 95% of native-PDF invoice line-item tables in the test set
TATR detects tables with IoU ≥ 0.85 on image-based documents
Table structure recognition achieves TEDS score ≥ 0.82 on benchmark
Empty cells are represented as null (not empty string) in output JSON
Multi-page tables are merged when continuation is detected
Bias evaluation performed
Model card generated

plan.description: Implement the FastAPI backend service. Wire all pipeline stages (OCR → NER → table extraction → JSON assembly) into the job execution flow. Implement async job submission with Celery + Redis as the task broker. Persist results to PostgreSQL via SQLAlchemy. Implement the review_and_critique agentic pattern: extraction output is scored by a critic agent before finalizing.

payload.job_flow: POST /extract → create job record → enqueue Celery task → return job_id → worker runs pipeline → critic scores output → if score < 0.7 retry with alternate engine → persist result → update job status
payload.critic_agent: Scores extraction completeness (required fields present), confidence distribution, and cross-field consistency (e.g. subtotal + tax ≈ total)
payload.serialization: Pydantic v2 models for all request/response types
payload.file_storage: Local filesystem (dev) / S3-compatible object store (prod) via abstracted StorageBackend

acceptance_criteria: POST /extract returns job_id within 200ms (async submission)
GET /jobs/{job_id}/result returns completed JSON within SLA after worker finishes
Critic agent re-routes to alternate OCR engine when initial confidence score < 0.7
All database writes use SQLAlchemy async session with proper transaction boundaries
API returns 422 with field-level detail for invalid input (wrong MIME type, file too large)
Concurrent extraction of 10 jobs does not cause race conditions in job status updates

plan.description: Implement batch processing subsystem. Accept up to 100 documents in a single multipart POST. Dispatch parallel Celery subtasks using a Chord (fan-out → fan-in). Track per-document status within batch. Emit batch_completed event when all subtasks resolve. Implement progress streaming via Server-Sent Events on GET /batches/{batch_id}/stream.

payload.concurrency: Worker pool size configurable via CELERY_CONCURRENCY env var (default: 4)
payload.sse_endpoint: GET /batches/{batch_id}/stream — emits {doc_index, status, progress_pct} as each doc completes
payload.partial_failure: Batch marked 'partially_completed' if ≥ 1 doc fails; failed docs include error detail in results
payload.rate_limiting: Max 3 concurrent batches per client IP via Redis token bucket

acceptance_criteria: Batch of 10 PDFs processes in parallel — wall-clock time ≤ 1.5x single-document time
SSE stream emits an event for each completed document with correct progress percentage
Batch result endpoint returns per-document status (completed | failed) for every document submitted
Partial batch failure does not block successful documents from returning results
Rate limiter returns 429 with Retry-After header when limit is exceeded

plan.description: Create Docker Compose stack for local development and a production-ready Dockerfile. Services: api (FastAPI), worker (Celery), redis (broker + result backend), postgres (job store), flower (Celery monitoring UI). Add GitHub Actions CI pipeline for lint, test, and Docker build on PR.

services.worker: Same image as api, celery worker entrypoint, GPU passthrough optional via NVIDIA runtime

payload.ci_pipeline: GitHub Actions: ruff lint → pytest → docker build --no-cache → push to GHCR on main
payload.health_checks: All services have HEALTHCHECK instructions; api depends_on postgres and redis health
payload.env_management: .env file for dev, Kubernetes Secrets / AWS Parameter Store for prod

acceptance_criteria: docker compose up --build starts all services and passes health checks within 60 seconds
API is reachable at http://localhost:8000 and returns 200 on GET /health
Flower UI is reachable at http://localhost:5555 and shows active workers
CI pipeline runs in < 5 minutes on a standard GitHub-hosted runner
docker compose down -v cleanly removes all containers and volumes
Production Dockerfile image size < 2 GB (multi-stage build)

plan.description: Write unit tests for all pipeline stages, NER models, table extraction logic, and API endpoint handlers. Use pytest with pytest-asyncio for async handlers. Mock external I/O (file storage, database). Target ≥ 85% line coverage.

payload.framework: pytest 8 + pytest-asyncio + pytest-cov + httpx (async test client)
test_modules: test_ocr_engine.py — quality scorer, engine routing, bounding box normalization
test_ner_layer.py — entity extraction for all 3 doc types, confidence thresholding, fallback activation
test_table_extractor.py — native PDF tables, image-based detection, header inference, multi-page merge
test_pipeline.py — end-to-end DocumentState flow through all stages
test_api_endpoints.py — POST /extract, GET /jobs, batch endpoints, error cases
test_critic_agent.py — completeness scoring, consistency checks, re-routing trigger
payload.fixtures: Sample invoice/receipt/contract PDFs and images in tests/fixtures/
payload.mocks: StorageBackend, database session, Celery task execution

acceptance_criteria: pytest --cov reports ≥ 85% line coverage across src/
All tests pass in CI (no flaky tests — each test is deterministic)
Each OCR engine is tested with both a clean and degraded sample document
NER tests assert specific entity types and values against labeled fixtures
API tests cover happy path, 422 validation errors, 404 not found, and 429 rate limit

plan.description: Write integration and end-to-end tests against a live Docker Compose stack. Test the full extraction pipeline with real PDFs for all three document types. Validate structured JSON output against expected entity schemas. Test batch processing with concurrent load.

test_types: e2e_invoice.py — upload invoice PDF, poll to completion, assert all required entity types present
e2e_receipt.py — upload receipt image, assert merchant name, total, date extracted
e2e_contract.py — upload contract PDF, assert party names, effective date, governing law
e2e_batch.py — submit 5-doc batch, assert all complete, verify SSE stream events
e2e_dedup.py — submit same file twice in one batch, assert second is deduplicated
load_test.py — 20 concurrent single-doc requests, assert all complete within 120s
payload.test_fixtures: 10 real-world anonymized samples per document type in tests/e2e/fixtures/
payload.environment: pytest with INTEGRATION_TEST=1 env var, spins up docker compose test profile

acceptance_criteria: All e2e tests pass against a freshly started Docker Compose stack
Invoice e2e asserts: VENDOR_NAME, INVOICE_NUMBER, TOTAL, DUE_DATE all present in result
Receipt e2e asserts: MERCHANT_NAME, TOTAL, TRANSACTION_DATE all present in result
Contract e2e asserts: at least 2 PARTY_NAME entities and EFFECTIVE_DATE present
Batch e2e confirms all 5 documents reach 'completed' status within 180 seconds
Load test: p95 latency < 45 seconds for single-page documents under 20 concurrent requests

plan.description: Perform security review of the document extraction API. Threat model: malicious file uploads, path traversal, SSRF via webhook callbacks, PII data leakage in logs, and unauthenticated batch job enumeration. Implement mitigations.

threat_areas: File upload: MIME type validation, magic byte verification, file size cap (default 50 MB), virus scan hook
Path traversal: all file paths resolved via pathlib.Path with chroot-style prefix validation
PII in logs: structured log scrubber — redact entity values from INFO-level logs, keep only entity types
Auth: API key authentication via X-API-Key header (no auth in dev mode only)
Job enumeration: job IDs are UUIDs v4, not sequential integers
Resource exhaustion: per-IP rate limiting already in step 9; add file count limit per API key
payload.tooling: bandit (static analysis), safety (dependency CVE scan), OWASP ZAP baseline scan in CI

acceptance_criteria: bandit scan reports zero HIGH severity findings in src/
safety check reports zero CRITICAL CVEs in requirements.txt
Uploading a file with .pdf extension but EXE magic bytes returns 422
Extracted entity values do not appear in INFO-level application logs
Unauthenticated request to any endpoint returns 401 when API key auth is enabled
OWASP ZAP baseline scan passes with no FAIL-level alerts

plan.description: Set up production monitoring and alerting. Instrument FastAPI with Prometheus metrics (request count, latency histograms, job queue depth, extraction success rate). Configure Grafana dashboard. Define SLO: 99% of single-document jobs complete within 60 seconds.

metrics: critic_rerout_total (counter — times critic triggered engine re-route)
alerting: ALERT: p95 extraction latency > 60s → PagerDuty P1

acceptance_criteria: GET /metrics endpoint returns valid Prometheus text format
All six metric families appear in Prometheus scrape output
Grafana dashboard JSON (exported) is committed to infra/grafana/
Alert rules are defined in infra/prometheus/alerts.yaml and load without syntax errors
Celery queue depth metric updates within 15 seconds of task enqueue

plan.description: Define and execute a QA test plan covering extraction accuracy benchmarks, edge cases, and regression suite. Produce a formal test report with pass/fail status per document type and entity class.

test_plan_sections: Accuracy benchmarks: F1, precision, recall per entity class per document type on 50-doc labeled set
Edge cases: scanned handwritten amounts, rotated pages, multi-column contracts, receipts with no tax line
Regression suite: 20 previously failed extractions, assert they now pass after model updates
Negative tests: password-protected PDFs, corrupted images, zero-byte files — all return structured error JSON
Localization edge: documents with € and £ currency symbols and non-US date formats
payload.output: QA report in docs/qa_report.md with per-entity F1 table and failure analysis

acceptance_criteria: QA report documents F1 ≥ 0.85 for all REQUIRED entity types across all document types
All negative test cases return HTTP 422 or 500 with structured error body (no unhandled exceptions)
Regression suite: 100% pass rate on previously known failure cases
Edge case results are categorized: handled gracefully | known limitation | bug (filed as GitHub issue)
QA report is reviewed and signed off by data_scientist and qa_engineer agent roles

plan.description: Write API documentation, developer quickstart guide, and model card for the extraction models. Document the agentic pipeline architecture (multi_agent_sequential + review_and_critique patterns) and how to extend it for new document types.

deliverables: README.md — quickstart: clone → docker compose up → first extraction in 5 minutes
docs/api_reference.md — all endpoints with curl examples and annotated JSON responses
docs/architecture.md — pipeline diagram (Mermaid), agentic patterns used, data flow
docs/model_card.md — OCR engines, NER model details, training data provenance, known limitations
docs/extending.md — how to add a new document type: labeled data → fine-tune → register entity types → add spaCy patterns
docs/deployment.md — production checklist: env vars, object storage config, GPU setup, scaling workers

acceptance_criteria: README quickstart works on a clean machine following only the documented steps
Every API endpoint has a working curl example in api_reference.md
Architecture Mermaid diagram renders correctly on GitHub
Model card documents training dataset size, evaluation metrics, and known failure modes
docs/extending.md includes a worked example adding a 'purchase_order' document type

flaws: Step 6 assumes a fine-tuned LayoutLMv3 exists ('fine-tuned on FUNSD + custom labeled invoices/receipts/contracts') but there is NO step in the plan that covers data labeling, model training, or fine-tuning. FUNSD is a generic form-understanding dataset — it will not hit F1 ≥ 0.88 on domain-specific invoices out of the box. This is the most critical gap in the entire plan.

Step 9 uses Celery Chord for fan-out and SSE for per-document progress streaming, but these are architecturally incompatible as described. A Chord callback fires ONCE when ALL subtasks complete, not incrementally per document. To emit per-document SSE events you need workers to write intermediate status to Redis/DB and the SSE endpoint to poll that — not a chord callback. This will not work as written.

Step 4 acceptance criterion: '10-page PDF in under 30 seconds on a 2-vCPU machine' is not achievable with EasyOCR + LayoutLMv3 + Table Transformer on CPU. EasyOCR cold-start alone is 5–15s; inference on 10 pages at 300 DPI (10 × ~5MB images) on CPU takes 60–120s. This SLA requires GPU and is not flagged as such.

Step 10 acceptance criterion: 'Docker image size < 2 GB (multi-stage build)' is not achievable. PyTorch (required by EasyOCR and transformers) is ~2 GB by itself. Add CUDA libs, Tesseract binaries, spaCy models, LayoutLMv3 weights (~450 MB), Table Transformer weights, and Python deps — the image will be 6–10 GB minimum. This constraint will be failed immediately.

Step 3 acceptance criterion: 'JSONB columns validated by a CHECK constraint against a JSON schema' — PostgreSQL CHECK constraints cannot perform JSON Schema validation natively. You would need a PL/pgSQL function or extension (pg_jsonschema). This is non-trivial and not mentioned.

Step 8: Celery workers are synchronous by default. Using SQLAlchemy async sessions (asyncpg) inside Celery tasks requires a dedicated event loop per task (asyncio.run()). Mixing FastAPI's async context with Celery's sync workers without explicit loop management causes 'Event loop is closed' errors in production. This is a common failure mode that requires explicit design.

Step 8 critic re-routing: 'if score < 0.7, retry with alternate engine' — the plan does not define what happens when both engines fail below threshold, or when EasyOCR is already the engine used (no alternate exists). This creates an infinite loop or silent failure path.

Step 9 rate limiting is IP-based ('per client IP'). This fails silently behind corporate NAT, VPNs, or CDNs where hundreds of users share one IP. Step 13 adds API-key-based auth, but these two rate-limiting strategies are never reconciled into a single coherent policy.

Steps 11–12 tests are designed before Step 13 auth is implemented. Every test against authenticated endpoints will need to be reworked after auth is added, or tests must mock auth from day one. The dependency ordering creates technical debt in the test suite.

EasyOCR downloads models (~1.5 GB) on first use at runtime. In a containerized environment this means the first job submission will block for minutes or fail with a timeout. The Dockerfile in Step 10 does not pre-bake model weights into the image.
suggestions: Insert a 'ML_DATA' step between Steps 1 and 6: define data labeling schema, collect 500+ annotated documents per type, fine-tune LayoutLMv3, and run evaluation. Without this, Step 6 F1 targets are aspirational, not achievable.

Replace the Celery Chord + SSE pattern: have each Celery subtask write status updates to a Redis hash keyed by batch_id, and have the SSE endpoint poll that hash every 1s. Remove the chord pattern entirely for batch progress tracking.
Add a GPU/CPU tiering note to the SLA: state '30 seconds on 2-vCPU with Tesseract-only path; 60 seconds with EasyOCR on CPU; GPU required for < 15s'. Separate the SLAs by code path.

Split the Docker image: create a 'cpu-slim' image (Tesseract + spaCy only, ~1.5 GB) and a 'gpu-full' image (+ PyTorch + EasyOCR + transformers). Most document pipelines can start with cpu-slim and upgrade selectively.
Pre-download all ML model weights into the Docker image during build (using a dedicated RUN layer after pip install) so workers start cold in < 30 seconds.

Add an explicit step for the document classifier: Step 4 mentions 'lightweight CNN or zero-shot LLM classifier' but neither path is designed. Zero-shot via an LLM adds ~2–5s latency and cost per document. A fine-tuned DistilBERT on 3 classes is 30ms inference and should be the primary path.
Add a webhook/callback endpoint: polling-only APIs force clients into polling loops. Add POST /extract with optional callback_url parameter — far more efficient for batch consumers.

Define a data retention and purge policy in Step 3. Extracted text from invoices and contracts is PII-dense. The DELETE /jobs/{job_id} endpoint must cascade-delete all derived artifacts (entities, tables, stored file).

Resolve the Celery serializer explicitly: set task_serializer='json', result_serializer='json', accept_content=['json'] in Celery config. Default pickle is a code execution vector if Redis is reachable from untrusted networks.
Add a 'decompression bomb' guard: a 100KB PDF can expand to gigabytes. Add a size check after pdf2image conversion, not just on the upload.
missing: Model training pipeline: data collection, annotation tooling (Label Studio or Prodigy), fine-tuning script, and model registry. Without this, LayoutLMv3 will not meet F1 targets.

OCR bounding-box format translation layer: pytesseract returns (left, top, width, height) in pixels; EasyOCR returns [[x1,y1],[x2,y1],[x2,y2],[x1,y2]]; LayoutLMv3 expects normalized [x0,y0,x1,y1] in 0–1000 range. The conversion logic is non-trivial and completely absent from the plan.
Graceful worker shutdown: in-flight Celery tasks during a rolling deploy or scale-down. No SIGTERM handler defined; jobs will be orphaned.
Password-protected PDF handling: mentioned only as a negative test in Step 15 but no pipeline handling is designed. Should return a structured error at ingestion stage, not propagate to OCR.

Multi-language support: Step 5 mentions 'language packs: eng + any configured locales' but there is no configuration surface, no locale detection, and no NER model selection per language. This is a stub that will break for any non-English document.

Memory pressure management: a 50 MB PDF converted at 300 DPI produces ~300 MB of PIL images. With 10 concurrent workers, this is 3 GB of in-memory image data. No worker memory cap or per-job memory limit is defined.
API versioning: no /v1/ prefix, no deprecation strategy. Breaking changes to the JSON output schema (e.g., entity type renames after model updates) will silently break consumers.
Encryption at rest: raw PDFs and extracted entities (names, financials, legal parties) are stored unencrypted. For any real deployment processing business documents this is a compliance gap.
Token/cost budget for LLM-based document classifier if used: no rate limit, no cost tracking, no fallback if the LLM endpoint is down.
security_risks: Multipart batch upload (Step 9) accepts up to 100 files at 50 MB each = 5 GB per request. File size validation in Step 13 fires after reading, not before. A single request can exhaust disk or memory before rejection. Enforce Content-Length header limit at the reverse proxy and per-file size limit during streaming ingestion.

Celery default serializer (pickle) allows arbitrary Python object deserialization. If Redis is exposed or compromised, a crafted task message achieves remote code execution on all workers. Must set task_serializer='json' explicitly — this is not mentioned anywhere in the plan.

The Redis instance in the Docker Compose stack has no password configured. In any environment where Redis is not 100% network-isolated (cloud VMs, shared dev environments), this is an unauthenticated RCE surface via Celery task injection.

PDF content can include embedded JavaScript, URI actions, and launch actions. Neither pdfplumber nor pdf2image is immune to malicious PDFs. No mention of sandboxing the PDF processing workers (e.g., seccomp profile, no-network container policy, or running in a gVisor/Firecracker VM).

PII leakage surface is broader than Step 13 addresses: extracted entity values will appear in Celery task arguments, Redis result backend, PostgreSQL extraction_results, and potentially in exception tracebacks. The log scrubber alone is insufficient — need field-level encryption or result backend TTL.

SSRF via webhook callback_url (not in this plan but a natural next feature): if a callback_url field is added later without SSRF protection, the extraction service becomes a proxy for internal network scanning.

Job UUID v4 prevents sequential enumeration but does not prevent a compromised API key from listing all jobs for all users. No tenant isolation or job ownership model is defined — any authenticated client can poll any job_id if they guess it.
final_review.summary: The plan is well-structured and covers all the right conceptual areas — the dependency graph is logical, acceptance criteria are mostly concrete, and the technology selections are defensible. However, it has several failure modes that will block production delivery, not just slow it down. The most critical: there is no model training step. The plan assumes a fine-tuned LayoutLMv3 materializes by Step 6, but data collection, annotation, and fine-tuning are absent from the entire 16-step plan — this alone could add 4–8 weeks to the timeline. Second, the Celery Chord + SSE architecture for batch progress is technically broken as described and will require a redesign when implemented. Third, the Docker image size constraint (< 2 GB) and the 30s CPU SLA are both physically impossible given the chosen ML stack; these will cause the Step 10 and Step 4 acceptance criteria to fail on day one. The security review in Step 13 is solid in intent but arrives too late — auth and input validation should be in place before Steps 11–12 integration tests, not after. For an MVP where ML model quality targets can be relaxed and the SLA is GPU-qualified, this plan could score in the low 60s; as written, targeting the stated acceptance criteria on CPU infrastructure, it scores 57.

flaws: Step 6 assumes a fine-tuned LayoutLMv3 exists ('fine-tuned on FUNSD + custom labeled invoices/receipts/contracts') but there is NO step in the plan that covers data labeling, model training, or fine-tuning. FUNSD is a generic form-understanding dataset — it will not hit F1 ≥ 0.88 on domain-specific invoices out of the box. This is the most critical gap in the entire plan.

Step 9 uses Celery Chord for fan-out and SSE for per-document progress streaming, but these are architecturally incompatible as described. A Chord callback fires ONCE when ALL subtasks complete, not incrementally per document. To emit per-document SSE events you need workers to write intermediate status to Redis/DB and the SSE endpoint to poll that — not a chord callback. This will not work as written.

Step 4 acceptance criterion: '10-page PDF in under 30 seconds on a 2-vCPU machine' is not achievable with EasyOCR + LayoutLMv3 + Table Transformer on CPU. EasyOCR cold-start alone is 5–15s; inference on 10 pages at 300 DPI (10 × ~5MB images) on CPU takes 60–120s. This SLA requires GPU and is not flagged as such.

Step 10 acceptance criterion: 'Docker image size < 2 GB (multi-stage build)' is not achievable. PyTorch (required by EasyOCR and transformers) is ~2 GB by itself. Add CUDA libs, Tesseract binaries, spaCy models, LayoutLMv3 weights (~450 MB), Table Transformer weights, and Python deps — the image will be 6–10 GB minimum. This constraint will be failed immediately.

Step 3 acceptance criterion: 'JSONB columns validated by a CHECK constraint against a JSON schema' — PostgreSQL CHECK constraints cannot perform JSON Schema validation natively. You would need a PL/pgSQL function or extension (pg_jsonschema). This is non-trivial and not mentioned.

Step 8: Celery workers are synchronous by default. Using SQLAlchemy async sessions (asyncpg) inside Celery tasks requires a dedicated event loop per task (asyncio.run()). Mixing FastAPI's async context with Celery's sync workers without explicit loop management causes 'Event loop is closed' errors in production. This is a common failure mode that requires explicit design.

Step 8 critic re-routing: 'if score < 0.7, retry with alternate engine' — the plan does not define what happens when both engines fail below threshold, or when EasyOCR is already the engine used (no alternate exists). This creates an infinite loop or silent failure path.

Step 9 rate limiting is IP-based ('per client IP'). This fails silently behind corporate NAT, VPNs, or CDNs where hundreds of users share one IP. Step 13 adds API-key-based auth, but these two rate-limiting strategies are never reconciled into a single coherent policy.

Steps 11–12 tests are designed before Step 13 auth is implemented. Every test against authenticated endpoints will need to be reworked after auth is added, or tests must mock auth from day one. The dependency ordering creates technical debt in the test suite.

EasyOCR downloads models (~1.5 GB) on first use at runtime. In a containerized environment this means the first job submission will block for minutes or fail with a timeout. The Dockerfile in Step 10 does not pre-bake model weights into the image.
suggestions: Insert a 'ML_DATA' step between Steps 1 and 6: define data labeling schema, collect 500+ annotated documents per type, fine-tune LayoutLMv3, and run evaluation. Without this, Step 6 F1 targets are aspirational, not achievable.

Replace the Celery Chord + SSE pattern: have each Celery subtask write status updates to a Redis hash keyed by batch_id, and have the SSE endpoint poll that hash every 1s. Remove the chord pattern entirely for batch progress tracking.
Add a GPU/CPU tiering note to the SLA: state '30 seconds on 2-vCPU with Tesseract-only path; 60 seconds with EasyOCR on CPU; GPU required for < 15s'. Separate the SLAs by code path.

Split the Docker image: create a 'cpu-slim' image (Tesseract + spaCy only, ~1.5 GB) and a 'gpu-full' image (+ PyTorch + EasyOCR + transformers). Most document pipelines can start with cpu-slim and upgrade selectively.
Pre-download all ML model weights into the Docker image during build (using a dedicated RUN layer after pip install) so workers start cold in < 30 seconds.

Add an explicit step for the document classifier: Step 4 mentions 'lightweight CNN or zero-shot LLM classifier' but neither path is designed. Zero-shot via an LLM adds ~2–5s latency and cost per document. A fine-tuned DistilBERT on 3 classes is 30ms inference and should be the primary path.
Add a webhook/callback endpoint: polling-only APIs force clients into polling loops. Add POST /extract with optional callback_url parameter — far more efficient for batch consumers.

Define a data retention and purge policy in Step 3. Extracted text from invoices and contracts is PII-dense. The DELETE /jobs/{job_id} endpoint must cascade-delete all derived artifacts (entities, tables, stored file).

Resolve the Celery serializer explicitly: set task_serializer='json', result_serializer='json', accept_content=['json'] in Celery config. Default pickle is a code execution vector if Redis is reachable from untrusted networks.
Add a 'decompression bomb' guard: a 100KB PDF can expand to gigabytes. Add a size check after pdf2image conversion, not just on the upload.
missing: Model training pipeline: data collection, annotation tooling (Label Studio or Prodigy), fine-tuning script, and model registry. Without this, LayoutLMv3 will not meet F1 targets.

OCR bounding-box format translation layer: pytesseract returns (left, top, width, height) in pixels; EasyOCR returns [[x1,y1],[x2,y1],[x2,y2],[x1,y2]]; LayoutLMv3 expects normalized [x0,y0,x1,y1] in 0–1000 range. The conversion logic is non-trivial and completely absent from the plan.
Graceful worker shutdown: in-flight Celery tasks during a rolling deploy or scale-down. No SIGTERM handler defined; jobs will be orphaned.
Password-protected PDF handling: mentioned only as a negative test in Step 15 but no pipeline handling is designed. Should return a structured error at ingestion stage, not propagate to OCR.

Multi-language support: Step 5 mentions 'language packs: eng + any configured locales' but there is no configuration surface, no locale detection, and no NER model selection per language. This is a stub that will break for any non-English document.

Memory pressure management: a 50 MB PDF converted at 300 DPI produces ~300 MB of PIL images. With 10 concurrent workers, this is 3 GB of in-memory image data. No worker memory cap or per-job memory limit is defined.
API versioning: no /v1/ prefix, no deprecation strategy. Breaking changes to the JSON output schema (e.g., entity type renames after model updates) will silently break consumers.
Encryption at rest: raw PDFs and extracted entities (names, financials, legal parties) are stored unencrypted. For any real deployment processing business documents this is a compliance gap.
Token/cost budget for LLM-based document classifier if used: no rate limit, no cost tracking, no fallback if the LLM endpoint is down.
security_risks: Multipart batch upload (Step 9) accepts up to 100 files at 50 MB each = 5 GB per request. File size validation in Step 13 fires after reading, not before. A single request can exhaust disk or memory before rejection. Enforce Content-Length header limit at the reverse proxy and per-file size limit during streaming ingestion.

Celery default serializer (pickle) allows arbitrary Python object deserialization. If Redis is exposed or compromised, a crafted task message achieves remote code execution on all workers. Must set task_serializer='json' explicitly — this is not mentioned anywhere in the plan.

The Redis instance in the Docker Compose stack has no password configured. In any environment where Redis is not 100% network-isolated (cloud VMs, shared dev environments), this is an unauthenticated RCE surface via Celery task injection.

PDF content can include embedded JavaScript, URI actions, and launch actions. Neither pdfplumber nor pdf2image is immune to malicious PDFs. No mention of sandboxing the PDF processing workers (e.g., seccomp profile, no-network container policy, or running in a gVisor/Firecracker VM).

PII leakage surface is broader than Step 13 addresses: extracted entity values will appear in Celery task arguments, Redis result backend, PostgreSQL extraction_results, and potentially in exception tracebacks. The log scrubber alone is insufficient — need field-level encryption or result backend TTL.

SSRF via webhook callback_url (not in this plan but a natural next feature): if a callback_url field is added later without SSRF protection, the extraction service becomes a proxy for internal network scanning.

Job UUID v4 prevents sequential enumeration but does not prevent a compromised API key from listing all jobs for all users. No tenant isolation or job ownership model is defined — any authenticated client can poll any job_id if they guess it.
history.summary: The plan is well-structured and covers all the right conceptual areas — the dependency graph is logical, acceptance criteria are mostly concrete, and the technology selections are defensible. However, it has several failure modes that will block production delivery, not just slow it down. The most critical: there is no model training step. The plan assumes a fine-tuned LayoutLMv3 materializes by Step 6, but data collection, annotation, and fine-tuning are absent from the entire 16-step plan — this alone could add 4–8 weeks to the timeline. Second, the Celery Chord + SSE architecture for batch progress is technically broken as described and will require a redesign when implemented. Third, the Docker image size constraint (< 2 GB) and the 30s CPU SLA are both physically impossible given the chosen ML stack; these will cause the Step 10 and Step 4 acceptance criteria to fail on day one. The security review in Step 13 is solid in intent but arrives too late — auth and input validation should be in place before Steps 11–12 integration tests, not after. For an MVP where ML model quality targets can be relaxed and the SLA is GPU-qualified, this plan could score in the low 60s; as written, targeting the stated acceptance criteria on CPU infrastructure, it scores 57.
